title: Chapter 2 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Basic Types in TypeScript!

questions.question: Which of these is the correct type for storing text in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: TypeScript uses the lowercase `string` keyword.

questions.question: Template strings (using backticks) allow you to easily embed variables inside strings.
questions.explanation: Backticks allow for syntax like `${myVariable}` directly inside the string.

questions.question: What happens if you type `firstName.` in VS Code when `firstName` is defined as a string?
options: It shows both string and number methods.
TypeScript instantly provides auto-complete suggestions only for string methods.
It automatically converts it to a number.
questions.explanation: Because TS knows the type is `string`, Intellisense only suggests valid string properties/methods.

questions.question: Which of these is a completely invalid assignment for a `string` variable?
questions.explanation: You cannot assign the number 100 to a variable strictly annotated as a `string`.

questions.question: How does TypeScript handle decimal numbers?
options: It uses the single `number` type for all numbers.
questions.explanation: In JS and TS, there is only one numeric type: `number`.

questions.question: The `number` type in TypeScript supports hexadecimal numbers.
questions.explanation: Yes, TS natively supports decimal, hex, binary, and octal numeric literals.

questions.question: If you define `let age: number = 25;`, what will TypeScript do if you attempt `age = 'old';`?
options: Change the type of age to string.
questions.explanation: Since the variable is explicitly typed as `number`, attempting to assign a string will trigger a type error.

questions.question: Which of these is the correct syntax to declare a boolean?
questions.explanation: The proper keyword in TypeScript is the lowercase `boolean`.

questions.question: In TypeScript, boolean variables can store "maybe" as a value.
questions.explanation: Booleans strictly enforce using only two values: `true` or `false`.

questions.question: What is the result type of `let isGreater = 10 > 5;`?
questions.explanation: Comparison operators always evaluate to a boolean (`true` or `false`).

questions.question: What is considered the 'escape hatch' type in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: The `any` type is the escape hatch that essentially disables TypeScript checking for a variable.

questions.question: It is highly recommended by developers to always use the `any` type everywhere.
questions.explanation: No! Using `any` defeats the purpose of static typing and should be avoided whenever possible.

questions.question: If a variable is typed as `any`, can you call non-existent functions on it without compiler errors?
options: Yes, the compiler will ignore it.
Yes, but it will automatically fix the function name.
No, the compiler will still throw an error.
No, the compiler will refuse to compile completely.
questions.explanation: Because it is `any`, TypeScript trusts you completely and won't throw compilation errors, though it will crash in the browser!

questions.question: Type _______ is when TypeScript automatically determines the type based on the value you assign.
questions.explanation: When you write `let x = 10;`, TypeScript uses Type Inference to know `x` is a number.

questions.question: What happens if you declare `let score;` and assign it a value on the next line?
options: TypeScript defaults it to `any` because it lacked an initial value.
TypeScript infers it as a number.
TypeScript infers it as a string.
questions.explanation: Because it wasn't given a value immediately, inference fails, and TS marks it as `any`.

questions.question: What type does TS infer for `let loggedIn = false;`?
questions.explanation: Since `false` is assigned right away, TS infers the variable type is `boolean`.

questions.question: You MUST add explicit type annotations to every single variable you create in TypeScript, even if it has an initial value.
questions.explanation: Type Inference often handles standard assignments cleanly, minimizing the need to explicitly write types everywhere.

questions.question: If I write `let player: string;` but forget to assign a value later, what is the major benefit of the type annotation?
options: It prevents anyone from assigning a number to `player` later.
It makes the app run 10x faster.
It gives the string a default value of 'Hello'.
questions.explanation: By explicitly declaring the type, TS will ensure only a string can *ever* go into that variable.

questions.question: Why is static typing useful in large enterprise codebases?
options: It forces you to write shorter code.
It catches tricky bugs early before deployment and acts as documentation.
It automatically formats HTML css logic.
It prevents you from using variables at all.
questions.explanation: Catching bugs instantly in the IDE saves thousands of hours of debugging and live crashes.

questions.question: The `any` type gives you the best editor autocompletion support.
questions.explanation: False. Because `any` could be absolutely anything, the editor cannot give you reliable auto-suggestions.
